text: Hi, my name is Abhishek Rao and I am an international student. I am from Nepal and I did my schooling from Gandhari boarding school where I did my grade 4 to grade 12 and I am currently in this class. So during our second semester in our physics course we had to make telescope and we made a telescope with 23% 23 times magnification and we also incorporated the functionality of changing the focal length by adjusting the knob and this stand that also helped us to change the height. Currently I am doing multiple projects for different courses. In IELTS project I am working with Professor Srikant in the IoT lab. Our problem statement is to find the botholes and make a road index that helps drivers and government authorities to classify roads and I am currently working on the hardware part of the site where I work with the data from NPU which are inertial measurement units and air quality sensors. Abhishek Rao, what changes did you find in yourself as you have been in India for two years? How do you think your personality has grown? To be frank I have always lived in a bubble so my parents were a little bit strict. So how did you live in a bubble? I was in Australia. I was equally far from home as I am in Punjab right now but most of the time I was inside the hostel. I am here for academic purposes and other things. I am here in the hostel. There isn't much change in how I interact with the outside world. But I have become more friendly to people. In Nepal when I was in Kandeky boarding school I didn't interact with people outside of my class. But because of the course and curriculum, because of all these project works and how the student life is structured here, I have been exposed to many people. So now I can frankly talk and collaborate with people as well. What is the one thing if you get a chance to change one thing in your life from the past? What it would be in the past? From the past? Are there any boundaries? No, there is no boundary. I wouldn't change anything from the past if I had any chance. Because I think the decisions that I have made till now has made me who I am. So I value my decisions. I know few decisions I have made. It's not the most optimal but I have learnt from them. If you talk about any incident that you made in your life that was not optimal or that you somehow regret. We would say now that I don't regret any decision of mine. But when you sit and think about it, you think that I couldn't have done this. What is that conversation? So after grade 10, we do have the option to choose a different school. So I stayed in the same school but sometime I think I should have moved to the capital city and gone for a different school. Which would have exposed me to various new things. Let's talk about your project, Telisper. What is this project all about? So as I mentioned earlier, this is the project that you caused for me. Yes, please. So it was a course project with Professor Dheeraj. We had to make a workable prototype out of everyday materials that are provided. So we took the PPC pipes of different diameters and used wooden rings that we cut through the machineries in our makerspace and we assembled a prototype. What technology did you use for this? We just used the Bi-convex fence and PPC pipes and a stand. Was there any instruction that was given to you or was it completely run by your customers? Are you talking about this? This same project. It was a course project so we got the reimbursement other than that. Could you please talk about this data mining the water table project of yours? Sure. And please focus on your role in this project as well. So this is also another course project for our machine learning and pattern recognition course. It is run by Professor Siddharth. We are currently participating in a competition where we have to solve a classification problem. The data set contains around 60,000 data points which has the entries for 60,000 water points which are hand pumps, gravity wells and motor pumps as well. Our task is to classify if the water points are functional, non-functional or functional but that need repair. Is there any... I was just trying to say what my part was. So me and my team, we used a random forest and my part was to do the exploratory data analysis. Could you explain what that is? Oh. How did you decide for data analysis? I think it's just the data analysis part. Okay. In that I mostly use Pandas, Seaborn to make graphs and find out what kind of data is. Is it heavily populated with categorical data or numerical data? And if there are categorical data, then what is the cardinality of it? Cardinality? Cardinality means if a column has 10 different types of categories, let's say animals, the cardinality would be 10. So after the data analysis, we found out that out of 41 data columns, 30 of them were categorical. And two of them had more than 10,000 data points, seven of them had around 2,000 plus categories, and the remaining had around 7 to 15 categories for each of the columns. So we used random forests because we had to import them. If we go for one-hot encoding, then our dimension would have exploded. So we used label encoding. And the good part about random forests is that it doesn't depend on the scale of the data. So it only checks less than or greater than. So it worked well for us. So we got an accuracy of 0.8148 percent, and this is 1 percent less than the top ranking one. And our current rank is 3,068 out of 70,000 participants. Could you please help me understand why is one-hot encoding used? One-hot encoding. It's the simplest kind of encoding. If you could identify one-hot encoding, it's used because it converts categorical variables into just binary vectors. Why is it used though? So I'll give you an example. Let's say a column has a fruit name and there are two fruits, oranges and apples. So it will create fruit, apple, and for each of the fruits that has apple, it will give one. And if it has orange, it will give zero, like the binary values. What if the data set of fruits again has apple, oranges and bananas? It will just add another column and give zero to bananas as well for the column of apples. It just has apples? So it will be 1, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1. Yes, for different fruits that have the name except apple, it will be 0, 0, 1. So in our case, because there were, like I said, more than 10,000 categories and some of them are 2,000, our data set would have exploded and it would have been in multiple GBs. Currently it's 102 MBs. So we use one with label encoding. Okay. What's the difference between label encoding and one-hot encoding? So label encoding does not increase the number of columns. Let's say if there are five different categories in a column, it would randomly assign them numbers. And let's say if there is an item called bench, wherever it finds bench in that column, it will assign the number x. So I have to do this? Yes. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay. Okay.
